message.content:
Call me Ishmael—cast adrift upon the frothing edge of the world, where the sea is a vast, salt-stung cathedral and every wave tolls like destiny. Aboard the Pequod, that creaking ark of whalebone and iron will, men of every creed and continent are bound together in a floating crucible, their souls bartered for oil and legend. Above them strides Captain Ahab, storm-eyed and ivory-limbed, a man harpooned not in flesh but in spirit, dragged ceaselessly across the mind’s black horizon by the ghost of pain.

Moby Dick, the great white whale, swims not merely through water but through myth—an albino thunderhead made flesh, the inscrutable sneer of the cosmos, gleaming with all the terror of a god who will not speak. Ahab hunts him as if to stab the very heart of fate, nailing his rage to the mast with a doubloon that glitters like a false sun. Around them, omens flutter and fall: prophecies whispered, compasses spinning madly, the ocean deepening into a mirror of man’s own abyss.

In the final maelstrom, ship and crew are swallowed whole, and only Ishmael remains—a solitary fragment of wreckage afloat on eternity’s dark, indifferent swell.
